coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots boosted structure stability of nickel cobalt layered double hydroxides nanosheets electrodeposited on carbon cloth for energy storage
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wenchao
Quan, Hongying
Chen, Dezhi
coredata.dc:description: Flexible energy storage devices have gained a wide concern in latest years owing to their portable and practical characteristics. However, the capacitance decay of flexible energy storage devices and short cycle life limit their development. To solve these problems, we develop a simple electrodeposition method to enhance the electrochemical activity and structural stability of the nickel cobalt layered double hydroxides (NiCoLDH) nanosheet arrays on carbon cloth (CC) by rational introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQD). Due to the induced effect and toughening effect of CQD, the obtained CQD modified NiCoLDH nanosheet arrays on CC electrode (CQD/NiCoLDH@CC) not only exposes more active sites of NiCoLDH nanosheets to improve their electrochemical capacity, but also makes their structure more stable to enhance their cycle life. The optimized electrode of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC can release a high specific capacitance of 1587.1 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and maintain 1281.2 F g−1 at 20 A g−1, as well as good cycling stability with 60.1% capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. The assembled flexible aqueous asymmetric CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC//activated CC supercapacitor shows a high energy density of 0.37 mWh cm−2 with the power density of 3.79 mW cm−2 at 5 mA cm−2. This work provides new insight into the designing of pseudocapacitive materials for energy storage.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots boosted structure stability of nickel cobalt layered double hydroxides nanosheets electrodeposited on carbon cloth for energy storage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flexible energy storage devices have gained a wide concern in latest years owing to their portable and practical characteristics. However, the capacitance decay of flexible energy storage devices and short cycle life limit their development. To solve these problems, we develop a simple electrodeposition method to enhance the electrochemical activity and structural stability of the nickel cobalt layered double hydroxides (NiCoLDH) nanosheet arrays on carbon cloth (CC) by rational introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQD). Due to the induced effect and toughening effect of CQD, the obtained CQD modified NiCoLDH nanosheet arrays on CC electrode (CQD/NiCoLDH@CC) not only exposes more active sites of NiCoLDH nanosheets to improve their electrochemical capacity, but also makes their structure more stable to enhance their cycle life. The optimized electrode of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC can release a high specific capacitance of 1587.1 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and maintain 1281.2 F g−1 at 20 A g−1, as well as good cycling stability with 60.1% capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. The assembled flexible aqueous asymmetric CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC//activated CC supercapacitor shows a high energy density of 0.37 mWh cm−2 with the power density of 3.79 mW cm−2 at 5 mA cm−2. This work provides new insight into the designing of pseudocapacitive materials for energy storage.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Portable and wearable electronic devices, such as flexible displays, curved smartphones, and foldable capacitive touchscreens, have been widely used in our life [1]. High energy density, high cycle stability and mechanical durability are essential factors of high-performance flexible energy storage devices [2]. Supercapacitors are a kind of energy storage devices with fast charging/discharging rate, high power density, long cycle life and environment-friendly [3], and show a significant application potential in flexible energy storage devices due to their high mechanical flexibility, excellent safety, and almost constant performance under a certain degree of deformation [4].

Currently, the electroactive materials commonly used for supercapacitors are double-layer carbon and pseudocapacitive metal oxide materials [5–9]. Among them, pseudocapacitive materials are favored by researchers because of their high specific capacitance resulting from their fast and reversible redox reactions [10]. Nickel cobalt layered double hydroxides (NiCoLDH) is a typical pseudocapacitive material with a unique layer structure and large layer spacing [11,12]. The synergistic effect of the two cations can provide a high specific capacitance for NiCoLDH [13]. However, the serious agglomeration and inherent low electrical conductivity of NiCoLDH restrict the ion and electron transport thus reducing their electrochemical properties [14]. In addition, during the electrochemical reaction, the insertion and deintercalation of ions lead to changes or even collapse of the microstructure, resulting in the poor cyclic stability of NiCoLDH [15]. To solve these problems, common methods including doping [16,17], modification [18–20] have been explored to promote the energy storage performance of NiCoLDH. For example, Ga-doped NiCoLDH, synthesized by hydrothermal method, showed a specific capacitance of 2012.5 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1 [21]. Hu et al. reported that the cyclability of NiCoLDH could be increased by the slight Zn2+ doping using an ultrafast electrodeposition process [22]. In addition, MXene modified NiCoLDH delivered a mass specific capacitance of 1207 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 and high cycle stability with 93% capacitance maintenance after 5000 cycles [23]. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) modified NiCoLDH (GQD/NiCoLDH) released a specific capacitance of 2220 F g−1 under 1 A g−1 and significantly enhanced cycling stability [24].

Carbon quantum dots (CQD) are promising active materials for energy storage on account of these advantages of abundant edge effects, chemical inertness, ease of preparation, and low cytotoxicity [25–27]. Recently, it has been proved that CQD could evidently improve the capacitance performance of NiCoLDH [18,28,29]. For example, Zhuang et al. [29] prepared CQD modified NiCoLDH nanorods vis hydrothermal approach, and the NiCoLDH-CQD showed an enhanced specific capacitance of 1814 Fg−1 at 1 Ag−1. Co-MOF embedded with CQD was used to fabricate CQD modified NiCoLDH nanosheet arrays on carbon cloth (CC) by solvothermal method, and the CQD/NiCoLDH@CC electrode delivered an ultrahigh specific capacitance of 2023 F g−1 at 5 mA cm−2 [18]. However, the currently reported methods for the preparation of CQD modified pseudocapacitive NiCoLDH are complicated, or the preparation conditions are harsh, such as hydrothermal, solvent thermal processes, which require high reaction temperatures. It is difficult to control the preparation process and realize the large-scale synthesis. Moreover, the mechanism by which CQD enhances the capacitive properties in pseudocapacitive materials is not yet clear. Therefore, it is still a challenge to find a mild and controllable preparation method and explore its enhancement mechanism.

In this work, we successfully prepared CQD modified NiCoLDH nanosheet arrays on CC (CQD/NiCoLDH@CC) by one-step electrodeposition method. The electrodeposition method is easy to operate and has a controlled mass loading on CC. CQD was well decorated on the NiCoLDH nanosheets by electrodeposition resulting in more electrochemically active sites on NiCoLDH. Furthermore, the microstructure of NiCoLDH nanosheet arrays on CC was successfully adjusted by the modification of CQD, which improved their mechanical stability and electrical conductivity, and suppressed the structural collapse of NiCoLDH caused by volume change during the charge/discharge cycle. Therefore, the optimized CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC flexible electrode yields high gravimetric specific capacitance of 1587.1 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1 and durable cycle stability (remaining 60.1% of initial capacitance after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1). It indicates that the rational modification of CQD can effectively tune the microstructure of pseudocapacitive electrode materials and enhance their electrochemical performance.

All reagents (Text S1) were used directly without further purification. A three-electrode system was used to fabricate NiCoLDH@CC and CQD/NiCoLDH@CC flexible electrodes. Before deposition, CC (1 cm× 1.5 cm) was pretreated by N2 plasma to increase hydrophilicity. The N2 plasma-treated CC, platinum flake (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm) and Ag/AgCl electrode were applied as the working electrode, the counter electrode, and the reference electrode, respectively. Electrodeposition solution was 100 mL of CQD aqueous solution (see Text S2), 1.4540 g Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 1.2442 g Ni(CH3COO)2 and 1.4552 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 1.2454 g Co(CH3COO)2. CQD/NiCoLDH@CC electrodes were obtained by constant potential deposition at −1.0 V under room temperature. Afterward, the obtained flexible electrodes were washed with deionized water and freeze-dried at -40 ℃ for 12 h. The prepared CQD/NiCoLDH@CC electrodes were labeled as CQD/NiCoLDH-1@CC, CQD/NiCoLDH-2@CC, CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC, CQD/NiCoLDH-4@CC and CQD/NiCoLDH-5@CC in the presence of 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.1 g of CQD, respectively. NiCoLDH@CC was prepared using the above approach in the absence of CQD. Scheme 1 displays the synthesis process of the flexible electrodes. The mass loading of CQD/NiCoLDH on CC for all flexible electrodes was 4.0 mg cm−2.

The morphology and structure of the active materials on the prepared flexible electrodes were characterized using scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM), powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), etc. The detail of characterization was shown in Text S3.

The electrochemical measurements were carried out on the CHI 760E (ChenHua, Shanghai, China) electrochemical workstation. The three-electrode system, including the working electrode (as-prepared flexible electrode with the active area of 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm), counter electrode (platinum flake with area of 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm), and reference electrode (Hg/HgO), was used to evaluate the capacitive properties of the obtained flexible electrodes, and the electrolyte was 6 M KOH aqueous solution.

The gravimetric specific capacitance (Cm, F g−1) and areal capacitance (Ca, F cm−2) of the flexible electrodes were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively.

where I, Δt, ∆V and m represents the galvanostatic discharge current (A), discharge time (s), potential window (V), and mass loading of active materials on CC (g), respectively. S (cm2) is the active area of the flexible electrode.

In addition, the asymmetry supercapacitor (ASC) device containing the obtained flexible electrode (positive electrode), activated CC (negative electrode, ACC, prepared in Text S4), a piece of fiberglass filter paper (the separator), and 6 M KOH (the electrolyte) was fabricated and tested in a two-electrode system. PVC film was used to seal the device and the thickness of the device was 1.7 mm. CHI 760E electrochemical workstation and CT2001A battery test system were used to evaluate the electrochemical performance of the device.

The areal capacitance (Ca, mF cm−2) of the ASC was calculated according to the Eq. (2), the energy density (Ea, mWh cm−2) and power density (Pa, mW cm−2) of the ASC were evaluated using the Eqs. (3) and (4).

Fig. 1 presents the structural characterization of the as-prepared flexible electrodes. As showed in Fig. 1a, two board diffraction peaks located at 25.8◦and 42.6◦ are attributed to the carbon materials of CC [30]. In addition, the main diffraction peaks of the NiCoLDH at 2θ of 9.5° correspond to (001) planes of the Co(OH)2 (JCPDS No. 51-1731). The diffraction peaks at 33.6◦ and 60.0◦ can be ascribed to the (012) and (110) planes of Ni(OH)2·0.75H2O (JCPDS No. 38-0715), respectively, which are match well with the reported results [31]. After CQD is added to the reactants, the absence of the peaks corresponding to Co(OH)2 may be due to its poor crystallinity and its weak diffraction peaks are covered by the peaks of CC. Moreover, the crystallinity of the samples gradually decreased as the concentration of CQD increased, which was reported to be beneficial to energy storage [6,9,32]. The successful modification of CQD into the NiCoLDH structure was confirmed by FTIR spectra (Fig. 1b). The broad band at 3445 cm−1 is assigned to the O-H stretching vibration of interlayer water molecules. The band at 1381 cm−1 can be ascribed to the interlayer NO3 − anions, which originated from the NO3 − in solution [33]. The band at 634 cm−1 is attributed to Ni/Co-O, indicating that the presence of NiCoLDH on CC [34,35]. After the introduction of CQD, the band appeared in 2343 cm−1 is assigned to CN [36]. As the concentration of CQD increased, the peak of CN gradually became apparent. It shows that CQD was successfully modified into the structure of NiCoLDH.

Fig. 2 presents the morphologies of the as-prepared electrodes observed by SEM. SEM image in Fig. 2a clearly shows that the numerous NiCoLDH nanosheets were uniformly electrodeposited on the surface of carbon fiber. According to the magnified SEM image in Fig. 2b, we can see that NiCoLDH nanosheets are homogeneously and densely arranged on the surface of carbon fiber. When the electrodeposition solution contained 0.1 g L−1 CQD, the electrodeposited NiCoLDH nanosheets on carbon fiber is still an even layer (Fig. 2c), and the magnified SEM image in Fig. 2d shows that these NiCoLDH nanosheets got flattened on the surface of CC after CQD modification. It may be mainly attributed to the decreased thickness of NiCoLDH nanosheets caused by CQD, which had been observed in our previous literature [18]. With the increase of CQD dosage in reaction solution to 0.25 g L−1, the uniformly electrodeposited layer can be observed in Fig. 2e, the orderly nanosheet arrays for the NiCoLDH were destroyed (Fig. 2f). When the concentration of CQD in the electrolyte reached 0.5 g L−1, the obtained NiCoLDH nanosheets are utterly and densely grown on the CC fibers (Fig. 2g). With the assistance of CQD, the deposited NiCoLDH was completely devoid of nanosheet structure as illustrated in Fig. 2h. It indicates that CQD can cause the transformation of NiCoLDH from nanosheet array structure to dense film-like structure on CC because the abundant functional groups at the edge of CQD, eventually reduced the crystallinity of NiCoLDH after the CQD modification, which is in accord with the XRD results. Compared with bare NiCoLDH, CQD/NiCoLDH-3 has a more compact structure, which is conducive to the electrode stability. As shown in Fig. 2i–l, the CQD/NiCoLDH-4 and CQD/NiCoLDH-5 on CC became denser and tiny particles appeared because excessive CQD during deposition can provide more abundant nucleation sites for NiCo-LDH. To further investigate the effect of CQD on the surface morphology of the material, the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. S2a) of bare NiCoLDH and CQD/NiCoLDH-3 were obtained at 77 K. The specific surface area and pore volume, calculated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and (Barret-Joyner-Halenda) methods, respectively, of NiCoLDH and CQD/NiCoLDH-3 are presented in Table S1. The calculated SBET of bare NiCoLDH and CQD/NiCoLDH-3 are about 1.7 and 2.7 m2 g−1, respectively. The calculate pore volume of NiCoLDH and CQD/NiCoLDH-3 are 0.023 and 0.030 cm3 g−1, respectively. The pore size distribution curves in Fig. S2b confirm that CQD did not change the pore size distribution significantly. The higher SBET and pore volume means the higher active sites and more convenient channel of CQD/NiCoLDH-3 for energy storage.

The morphology and structure of the bare NiCoLDH and CQD/NiCoLDH-3 were observed by TEM. Fig. 3 a exhibits the typical TEM image of NiCoLDH, which clearly indicates that the NiCoLDH consists of nanosheets, matching well with the SEM result. It can also be seen that there are obvious gaps between the nanosheets. It is clear to observe the lattice flow patterns from the high-resolution TEM images, as shown in Fig. 3b–d. The fringe spacing of 0.17 and 0.22 nm match very well with (110) planes of the Ni(OH)2⋅0.75H2O and (012) crystal of Co(OH)2, respectively. Additionally, the high angle angular dark field-scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and EDS mapping analysis (Fig. 3e) display the even distribution of Co, Ni, and O elements in NiCoLDH nanosheets. TEM image (Fig. 3f) of CQD/NiCoLDH-3 exhibits a dense hierarchical structure. CQD can adjust the structure of NiCoLDH from nanosheets to hierarchical structure, implying the presence of an induce effect of CQD, which is in keeping with the SEM results. This tightly hierarchical structure is conducive to facilitating ion storage and electrode stability. Fig. 3g–j show the HRTEM image of CQD/NiCoLDH-3 sample. We can observe that CQD are relatively uniformly dispersed in the NiCoLDH (Fig. 3g), and the spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (101) plane of crystalline carbon. Besides, the interplanar spacing of 0.24 and 0.17 nm are attributed to the (012) plane of the Ni(OH)2·0.75H2O and (110) plane of Co(OH)2, respectively. It indicates the good anchoring between CQD and NiCoLDH nanosheets, which can significantly improve the exposure of active sites for energy storage [37,38]. HAADF-STEM and EDS mapping image (Fig. 3k) of CQD/NiCoLDH-3 nanosheets demonstrate the uniform distribution of Ni, Co, O, N, and C elements in these nanosheets. The contents of elements in bare NiCoLDH and CQD/NiCoLDH-3 are presented in Table S2. There is no obvious difference in the contents of Ni and Co elements for bare NiCo LDH and CQD/NiCoLDH. The proportion of Ni and Co elements in bare NiCo LDH and CQD/NiCoLDH is near to 1. TG curve (Fig. S1) indicates that the CQD mass percentage in CQD/NiCoLDH-3 was about 1%.

XPS was applied to further determine the surface chemical composition and valence state of the active materials. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, the survey XPS spectra show the main elements of Ni, Co, O, and C in the as-prepared electrodes, and the presented N signals at around 400 eV is attributed to the CQD in the CQD/NiCoLDH-3. The contents of elements on the surface of bare NiCo LDH and CQD/NiCoLDH analyzed by XPS are shown in Table S3. Compared to bare NiCoLDH, the proportion of Ni and Co on the surface of CQD/NiCoLDH-3 is closer to 1, which is beneficial for the energy storage [39]. For high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p3/2 (Fig. 4b), the main peak at 779 eV and its satellite peak at 784.5 eV can be seen. Furthermore, the main peak at 779 eV can be fitted to two peaks of 781.0 and 778.8 eV, indicating the existence of Co2+ and Co3+ in the NiCoLDH [40]. Fig. 4c presents the XPS spectra of Ni 2p3/2, including the main peak at 854 eV and its satellite peak at 859.8 eV. The main peak can be deconvoluted into two species of Ni2+ and Ni3+ at 853.4 and 855.4 eV [41]. Compared with the bare NiCoLDH, the content of Co2+ and Ni3+ in CQD/NiCoLDH-3 is higher, which may be attributed to the strong affinity between Co2+/Ni3+ ions and O/N containing groups at the edge of CQD. The higher proportion for Co2+ and Ni3+ will be conducive to the energy storage of NiCoLDH [41]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) of bare NiCoLDH can be resolved into two peaks at 528.9 and 530.1 eV, which are related to metal-O and OH, respectively. After the modification of CQD, the C-O at 531.8 eV, which originated from CQD, can be observed in the O1s spectra of CQD/NiCoLDH-3.

Fig. 5 a shows the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of the as-prepared flexible electrodes under the scan rate of 0.5 mV s−1. We can observe the typical pseudocapacitance characteristics with a pair of redox peaks at around 0.25 and 0.15 V, which is mainly attributed to the fast and reversible redox reaction of Co2+/Co3+/Co4+ and Ni2+/Ni3+ on the surface of NiCoLDH [42,43].

Moreover, a new pair of intense redox peaks at about 0.33 V and 0.23 V for the CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC may be due to the reversible redox reactions Co3+/Co4+ [44]. XRD patterns at prominent redox peaks are presented in Fig. S3. The diffraction peaks of NiOOH and CoOOH can be observed during CV cycling. XRD results of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC at 0.33 V show different peaks from that of NiCoLDH@CC and the characteristic peaks of CoO2 can be observed. This may be attributed to that more Co3+/Co4+ transitions occurred on the surface of CQD/NiCoLDH-3, indicating that the introduction of CQD could improve the electrochemical activity of NiCoLDH.

Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurement was performed to further analyze the electrochemical properties of the obtained electrodes. Fig. 5b demonstrates the GCD curves of as-prepared flexible electrodes at 1 A g−1. Obvious potential platforms around 0.2 V can be found in all samples. With the modification of CQD, the discharge time for CQD/NiCoLDH@CC electrodes was lengthened, indicating the corresponding increase of specific capacitance, and the CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC electrode showed the highest specific capacitance in all electrodes. However, with the increase of CQD concentration to 0.75 and 1.00 g L−1, the specific capacitance for the obtained electrodes decreased or was even lower than the NiCoLDH@CC electrode. It indicates that suitable CQD modification has a positive impact on the electrochemical performance of the NiCoLDH, but excessive CQD is harmful to NiCoLDH. The effect of CQD on the electrochemical properties of as-prepared electrodes was investigated by the EIS measurement. Based on the simulated equivalent circuit (Fig. S4), the obtained Nyquist plots are displayed in Fig. 5c, all plots reflect an arc at the high and medium frequency. The intersection at the X-axis (Z') means the internal/bulk resistance (Rs ) of the electrode. The diameter of the arc represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct ) of the electrode, which is highly rely on the diffusion of the charges in electrode/electrolyte interface [45]. The obtained values of Rs and Rct are listed in Table S4, no obvious difference for the Rs of all as-prepared flexible electrodes suggests that the modification of CQD could not decrease the Rs of electrodes due to the low load of CQD or the electron-trap effect caused by the excessive functionalization of CQD edge sites [46]. However, the significantly reduced Rct for CQD/NiCoLDH@CC electrodes prepared using 0.10, 0.25 and 0.50 g L−1 CQD indicates that their charge transfer was improved, which is mainly because the proper densification of NiCoLDH nanosheet arrays induced by the moderate CQD. In all electrodes, the CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC showed the lowest Rct , indicating the fast charge transfer rate. The EIS plots of CQD/NiCoLDH-1@CC, CQD/NiCoLDH-2@CC, CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC and CQD/NiCoLDH-4@CC electrodes showed higher slope at the low frequency region, indicating that their ion diffusion is easier than other electrodes. The EIS results suggest that the appropriate amount of CQD can effectively improve the charge transfer and ion diffusion of NiCoLDH electrodes resulting in the enhanced electrochemical performance. However, excessive CQD is rather detrimental to the electrochemical performance of electrodes because the excessively dense structure induced by CQD inhibits the penetration of electrolyte in the NiCoLDH layer on the surface of CC.

For a deeper understanding of the charge storage process of NiCoLDH nanosheets in the absence and presence of CQD, Figs. S5 and 5d show the CV curves of NiCoLDH@CC and CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC at various scan rates of 0.1∼10 mV s−1, respectively. As the increasing of scan rate, the redox peaks gradually move to both sides of the potential window because of the presence of internal resistance of the electrodes [47]. A linear fit between the peak current and the scan rate (v 1/2) through the CV curve is shown in Fig. S6. The high linear correlation coefficients (R2, 0.952 and 0.979) for NiCoLDH@CC indicate that the charge storage of bare NiCoLDH is predominated by a diffusion-controlled behavior [48]. The low coefficients (R2, 0.850 and 0.904) for CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC suggest that the capacitive-controlled behavior is enhanced after the modification of CQD, which is favorable for the fast charge storage of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC. In addition, the Power-law was also utilized to evaluate the capacitive contribution of NiCoLDH nanosheets in the absence and presence of CQD (Text S5) [49,50]. The calculated b value from the redox peaks in Figs. S5 and 5d can be used to understand the charge storage process of the electrodes. The value of b is 1 or 0.5 representing capacitive-controlled or diffusion-controlled behavior. The calculated b values (Fig. S7) for the NiCoLDH@CC and CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC electrodes were 0.731 and 0.763, respectively. The results show that the dynamics of energy storage in the two electrodes were controlled by both the capacitive and diffusion-controlled behavior [51,52]. The ratio of the contribution of capacitive and diffusion-controlled was further estimated using the modified Power-law, illustrated in Figs. S8, 9. It is observed from Fig. 5e that the proportion of contribution from capacitive control of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC is more than that of NiCoLDH@CC, indicating that CQD modulates the NiCoLDH structure to promote charge diffusion on the surface of active material. At a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1, CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC showed a higher capacitive contribution of 67.9%, while the diffusion control behavior was 32.1%, indicating the fast reversible charge storage of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC. Because of the limitation of diffusion time, the diffusion-controlled capacitance of electrodes decreases as the increasing of scan rate [31]. Figs. 5f and S10 present the GCD curves of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC and NiCoLDH@CC at various current densities, respectively. The GCD plots with obvious voltage platforms demonstrate the battery-type behavior of NiCoLDH. The calculated specific capacitances of as-prepared flexible electrodes based on the galvanostatic discharge curves are displayed in Fig. 5g. The specific capacitance of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC is 1587.1 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, which is highest in all electrodes. The capacitance retention of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC electrode is up to 80.8% at 20 A g−1, suggesting its excellent rate capability. Notably, the CQD/NiCoLDH-1@CC and CQD/NiCoLDH-2@CC also show good rate capability with 78.8 and 79.4% capacitance retention at 20 Ag−1. However, the capacitance retention of CQD/NiCoLDH-4@CC and CQD/NiCoLDH-5@CC are 74.2% and 65.9% at 20 Ag−1, which is lower than NiCoLDH@CC (77.9%). The impressive results confirm that the rational modification of CQD can enhance the capacitive performance of NiCoLDH nanosheets. The mass loading of the active material and the specific capacitance of the CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC electrode were plotted in comparison with previous reports as shown in Fig. 5h and Table S4. It indicates that CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC delivers a high specific capacitance under high mass loading of 4 mg cm−2. It is mainly attributed to the face that the appropriate concentration of CQD modified on NiCoLDH can not only increase active redox sites for NiCoLDH nanosheets to increase their specific capacitance, but also well improve the diffusion of ions in NiCoLDH nanosheets to accelerate the dynamics of energy storage.

A continuous GCD test was carried out at 10 A g−1 to evaluate the cycle stability of the NiCoLDH-based electrodes prepared in the absence and presence of CQD and the results are shown in Fig. 5i. 60.1% of initial capacitance can be remained for the CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC after 10,000 cycles, better than that of NiCoLDH@CC (50.7%), suggesting that the moderate modification of CQD can enhance the cycle stability. To confirm the effect of CQD on the cycle stability of as-prepared flexible electrode, Fig. 6 presents the SEM images of the NiCoLDH@CC and CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC after 10,000 cycles. As shown in Fig. 6a, b, after 10,000 cycles, the surface of bare NiCoLDH on CC was severely deformed and accompanied by peeling, and the original NiCoLDH nanosheet arrays (Fig. 2a, b) were converted into submicrospheres after a long-term cycle, which is easy to fall off from the surface of CC (Fig. S11a, b). The schematic diagram for the formation of submicrospheres is presented in Fig. 6c. The loose bare NiCoLDH nanosheet arrays on CC are converted into these ultrathin nanosheets during the phase transition process between Co/NiOOH and Co/Ni(OH)2 or CoO2. These ultrathin nanosheets are prone to curl up and form submicrospheres. These submicrospheres are responsible for the bad cycling structural stability of the electrode. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 6d, e, CQD/NiCoLDH-3 nanosheet arrays still cover the surface of CC very well and show excellent structural stability after 10,000 cycles. It is mainly attributed to that the abundant O/N containing groups at the edge of CQD could strongly interact with Ni/Co ions to form the toughening effect, which can effectively release the stress caused by volume change during cycling tests [24]. In addition, because of the tight arrangement in the CQD/NiCoLDH-3 nanosheets, there are no space for these generated ultrathin nanosheets to curl up and form spherical aggregation (Fig. 6f), so the CQD/NiCoLDH-3 nanosheet arrays are remained on CC. After long-term cycle of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC, it is unchanged in the electrolyte and no active material dropped at the bottom of the test cell (Fig. S11c, d). The excellent structural stability of NiCoLDH on CC is beneficial to obtaining high cycle stability for the as-prepared flexible electrodes. The structural stability of the CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC electrode was further confirmed by TEM. Fig. 7 shows the TEM and EDS results of CQD/NiCoLDH-3 nanosheets after 10,000 cycles. The compact arrangement of these ultrathin NiCoLDH nanosheets interlaced with each other can ensure their stable anchor on the surface of CC, resulting in excellent structural stability and good cycle stability. HRTEM images of CQD/NiCoLDH-3 nanosheets (Fig. 7 b–e) display the clear lattice stripes with lattice spacing of 0.24 and 0.18 nm, which are assigned to (012) plane of Ni(OH)2⋅0.75H2O and (110) plane Co(OH)2, respectively. Besides, the lattice stripes of CQD with the spacing of 0.21 nm can still be observed, suggesting the critical role of CQD in improving the cycle stability of the electrode. HAADF-STEM and element EDS mapping (Fig. 7f) results show that the Co, Ni, O, C and N elements are evenly distributed in the nanosheets, further demonstrating the robust durability of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC electrode after 10,000 cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of CQD/NiCoLDH-3s@CC based ASC
ce:section.ce:para: The asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device was fabricated with CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC and ACC, 6 M KOH aqueous solution as the positive electrode, negative electrode, and electrolyte, respectively, and denoted as CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC//ACC ASC. Fig. S12 presents the electrochemical properties of ACC. The ACC has a voltage window between -1 and 0 V and exhibits a large double layer capacitance (Fig. S12a). The obtained areal capacitance of ACC is up to 1.2 F cm−2 at 5 mA cm−2 and remains at 0.96 F cm−2 at 50 mA cm−2 (Fig. S12b), and the capacitance retention is 80%. According to the CV area shown in Fig. S12c, the ratio for the active area of ACC and CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC is 2. As shown in Fig. 8 a, there is no significant polarization in the CV curve of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC//ACC ASC when the voltage window is between 0 and 1.5 V, so the voltage window of 1.5 V was chosen, which is further confirmed in Fig. 8b. Fig. 8c exhibits the CV curves of CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC//ACC ASC under various scan rates from 5 to 30 mV s−1, suggesting the presence of both electric double-layer and battery-type capacitance. Moreover, no obvious change in the geometry of the CV curve with the increase of scan rate, indicating the excellent rate capability of the assembled ASC. Fig. 8d displays the GCD curves at various current densities. The approximately triangular and good symmetry suggests the good rate capability and high Coulombic efficiency. Fig. 8e presents the plot of the areal capacitance versus current density of the assembled ASC. At the current densities of 5 and 30 mA cm−2, the calculated areal capacitance of the device was 1.17 and 0.78 F cm−2, respectively. As presented in Fig. 8f, the assembled ASC remained 55.5% of initial capacity and the corresponding Coulombic efficiency was 100% after 10,000 cycles. Ragone plot (Fig. 8g) demonstrates the calculated energy density (Ea) and power density (Pa) of the assembled ASC. When the current density was 5 mA cm−2, the calculated Ea and Pa were 0.37 mWh cm−2 and 3.79 mW cm−2, respectively. The Ea and Pa was 0.23 mWh cm−2 and 22.50 mW cm−2 at 30 mA cm−2, respectively. The assembled device delivered improved energy density/power density compared to these latest LDH-based ASCs (Table S5), such as NiCoLDH@rGO//AC (0.08 mWh cm−2 at 2.56 mW cm−2) [53], Co(OH)F@NiCoLDH//RGO (0.22 mWh cm−2 at 2.40 mW cm−2) [54], NiCoFeLDH//AC (0.18 mWh cm−2 at 0.24 mW cm−2) [55], HA-NiCoLDH-1//AC (0.10 mWh cm−2 at 0.76 mW cm−2) [56], PNT@NiCoLDH//AC (0.19 mWh cm−2 at 2.40 mW cm−2) [57]. CC@MOF-74(NiO)@NiCoLDH//CC@NiCo2O4@NiFeLDH (0.19 mWh cm−2 at 14.87 mW cm−2) [15]. In addition, one 2 cm× 3 cm size CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC//ACC ASC can continuously power the hour clock (Fig. 8h, Video S1). In addition, the electrochemical properties of ASC were tested at different bending angles (denoted as bent 1–60◦, 2–90◦ and 3–120◦) and bending times (denoted as bent 4-bending 300 times). The obtained CV and GCD curves are shown in Fig. 8i and Fig. S13, respectively. The nearly overlapping curves indicate that the excellent flexibility and stability for the ASC. Owing to the high energy density, good cycling stability, and excellent flexibility, CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC electrodes show potential of application in wearable energy storage device.

In this work, the CQD modified NiCoLDH nanosheets were successfully grown on CC by one-step electrodeposition. The moderate introduction of CQD not only improves the redox activity of NiCoLDH, but also optimizes the structure of NiCoLDH. The obtained CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC electrodes delivered an extraordinary specific capacitance of 1587.1 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1, significantly improved rate capability and cycle stability. Furthermore, the assembled asymmetric CQD/NiCoLDH-3@CC//ACC ASC device delivered an energy density of 0.37 mWh cm−2 with the power density of 3.79 mW cm−2 at 5 mA cm−2. The impressive results indicate that the prepared electrode displays immense potential in flexible energy storage device, and the decorated strategy of CQD for pseudocapacitive material by a simple one-step electrodeposition provides some new insights for fabricating high performance flexible electrodes.

Wenchao Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Hongying Quan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Dezhi Chen: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.
